text: The surgeon makes an incision in the right triangular ligament to mobilize the liver. The duodenum is then caucarized, and the inferior vena cava (IVC) is skeletonized from the posterior peritoneum. The right gonadal vein and right ureter are identified and carefully dissected.
The surgeon identifies the right accessory lower pole renal artery, dissects it, applies clips, and divides it. Subsequently, the Gerota's fascia surrounding the right renal vein is dissected.
The surgeon dissects the left border of the inferior vena cava (IVC), carefully identifying, ligating, and dividing the individual lumbar veins.

The surgeon frees the infrarenal portion of the inferior vena cava (IVC) and loops it using a VESI loop. Posterior lumbar tributaries are identified, clipped, and divided to prevent bleeding. The left renal vein is dissected, looped, and secured with a VESI loop. The right renal vein, containing the thrombus, is carefully dissected and secured, ensuring all major and minor tributaries to the IVC are managed to prevent intraoperative hemorrhage.
The surgeon visualizes the right renal artery in the retrocaval region, clips it without division, and carefully secures the short and fragile right adrenal vein to prevent potential bleeding.

The surgeon prepares to access the retrohepatic portion of the inferior vena cava (IVC) by carefully dissecting the short hepatic veins and the caudate lobe of the liver, avoiding the use of electrosurgical diathermy.

The surgeon reflects the caudate lobe away from the inferior vena cava (IVC), providing a clear view of the anatomical structures. An intraoperative ultrasound is used to evaluate the superior and inferior limits of the thrombus within the IVC.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic Satinsky's clamp to secure the infrahepatic portion of the inferior vena cava (IVC), performing a dry run to ensure proper placement. The narrow surgical space is visible, highlighting the importance of using the Satinsky's clamp over a bulldog clamp.

The surgeon establishes complete control around the inferior vena cava (IVC) using Bulldog clamps proximally and a Satinsky's clamp on the retrohepatic segment. The left renal vein is secured with a Bulldog clamp, while intravenous mannitol is administered to the patient.

The surgeon performs a cavotomy at the convergence of the right renal vein and inferior vena cava (IVC), extending the incision cranially to reach the upper limit of the tumor thrombus. Despite control over the IVC, there is visible oozing at the incision site. The superior limit of the tumor thrombus is delivered, revealing the orifice of the right hepatic vein as the upper boundary.
The surgeon extends the cavotomy circumferentially to include the cuff of the IVC and right renal vein, ensuring precise incision around these structures.

The surgeon begins closure of the cavatomy from its superior limit using a continuous suture with 5-0 Prolene. Robotic instruments, including ProGrass forceps, are used to handle the cavatomy edges, while monitoring clamp time and the patient's vital parameters.
The surgeon applies gentle pressure on the inferior vena cava (IVC) using ProGrass forceps, leaving visible indentations from the sharp teeth of the instrument on the delicate structure.

The surgeon completes the cavotomy closure with the final sutures while the surgical assistant inserts an eight French infant feeding tube pre-filled with normal saline into the cavotomy. The assistant infuses normal saline into the inferior vena cava (IVC) to eliminate any remaining air bubbles, clots, or thrombi.
The surgeon performs a watertight closure of the cavotomy, considering the thin-walled inferior vena cava (IVC) by employing a two-layered closure technique.
The surgeon releases the clamps in a cranial to caudal order. The right renal artery is clipped and divided on the right side of the inferior vena cava (IVC).
The surgeon performs a hilar lymphadenectomy, ensuring proper hemostasis before placing the specimen into a retrieval bag. The specimen is then extracted through a fan and steel incision.
